figures.caption: Figure 6: Scoring-context ablation on Kinetix (success rate %, 12 tasks × 1024 rollouts). Top: a matched-context variant scoring each action under its own generating context (rθ(A+) at cfresh, rθ(A −) at cstale) uniformly underperforms the unified-cmix DEFLECT recipe across all 8 inference delays. Bottom: the gap ∆ grows monotonically from −0.3 at d=0 to −2.3 at d=7, precisely the regime where cmix differs most from either cfresh or cstale.
Figure 7: Kinetix horizon robustness at d=1. Success rate vs. execution horizon K ∈ {1, . . . , 8} at fixed delay d=1. DEFLECT’s improvement is consistent across K.

Figure 2: Async-VLA inference under deployment-relevant latency: a real-robot example. A robot dispenses pellets from a small container into a target vial on a moving conveyor belt (a pharmaceutical-filling analog). Top: four robot frames at the timeline points marked below. Bottom: two consecutive VLA inference cycles. The policy commits to actions at inference starts, when the vial is at the target pour location; by execution starts the vial has moved several centimeters along the belt; the actions predicted from the earlier scene now release pellets where the vial was, not where it is — pellets scatter onto the belt and the task fails. This prediction-execution misalignment is intrinsic to async control under non-trivial inference latency.
Figure 4: Kinetix delay robustness. Success rate vs. inference delay d ∈ {0, . . . , 7} at K=max(d, 1). Horizon-robustness sweep and full per-delay numbers in Appendix E.

Figure 12: What DEFLECT does to the flow-matching policy, aggregated across all 12 Kinetix tasks at d=6. (a) Intervention magnitude is dynamics-aware: contact-rich transient tasks (grasp easy, trampoline, launch-family, mean 0.37−0.60) receive the largest corrections; periodic locomotion gaits (mjc walker, mjc half cheetah, 0.15−0.18) the smallest. (b) Variance is preserved: per-task σ-ratios cluster tightly around 1 (median 0.97, all 12 tasks within [0.81, 1.14]).

Figure 13: Mean correction magnitude vs. chunk position (aggregated over 12 tasks × 4 critical states at d=6). Approximately uniform across the 8-position predicted chunk, with a mild emphasis on positions 0−2.

Table 12: Kinetix delay: restart-effect decomposition (success rate %). ∆restart isolates the cosine/optimizer-restart effect (VLASH-RETR. − VLASH); ∆DPO isolates the net contribution of preference alignment (DEFLECT − VLASH-RETR.). Both columns are positive on average, and ∆DPO grows monotonically with d from ≈ 0 at low delay to +2.3 at d=6, consistent with the stalevs-fresh-contrast argument.

Figure 5: Real-robot results. (a) Full-task success rate on two conveyor pick-and-place tasks of increasing difficulty. (b) Mean moles struck per 30-second trial on the reactive Whack-a-Mole task. Across all three tasks, DEFLECT matches or improves on VLASH in the latency-sensitive regime.

Figure 3: DEFLECT framework. Steps 1–4 (offline training): (1) A frozen reference VLA produces preferred/rejected pairs from counterfactual fresh/stale contexts; (2) both are scored under the single deployment-time context cmix; (3) the calibrated DPO margin is formed; (4) the total loss updates the policy. Step 5 (online inference): the tuned VLA reads cmix and runs the same ODE as the base policy — no additional cost. Photos show cfresh, cstale, cmix on the Conveyor-II task.
Figure 11: Sensitivity to λDPO on Kinetix. Robust over λ ∈ [0.01, 0.05]; the final choice λ=0.02 maximizes the joint (delay-avg, horizon-avg) objective.

Figure 10: Ablations on Kinetix (delay-averaged success rate % over d ∈ {0, . . . , 7}). Five bars from left to right: VLASH baseline (79.4); λDPO=0, i.e. continued SFT only with our cosine schedule (82.0, isolates the cosine-restart effect); DPO with the VLASH-style partially-corrected rejection rather than naive-stale (81.9); DPO without the expert SFT anchor (catastrophic collapse to 12.3); and the full DEFLECT recipe at λDPO=0.02 (83.3).
Figure 8: LIBERO 4-suite average success rate under varying inference delay. Gains grow from +0.2 at d=1 to +4.6 at d=7.

Table 6: Statistical uncertainty for Table 4. Each entry is the half-width (in percentage points) of the 95% Wilson binomial confidence interval around the corresponding success rate in Table 4, computed from N=500 episodes. For example, a half-width of ±1.5 pp means a reported success rate of X% has 95% CI [X−1.5, X+1.5]. All half-widths are below ±4.3 pp and the majority are below ±3.0 pp, so the +4.6 headline gain at d=7 exceeds any per-cell CI.

Table 8: PFM vs. DEFLECT on Kinetix (success rate %). PFM passes the sanity check at d=0 (+0.8 over VLASH, the clean-observation regime where its preference supervision is valid) but is a negative-transfer correction at d ≥ 2: under stale observations, integrating a flow trained on cleanobservation preferences produces worse actions than the base VLASH policy. DEFLECT beats the strongest PFM variant by +4.9 on delay-averaged success and by +8.0 on the extreme-delay average, confirming that the delay-aware preference signal is the component that turns preferencealignment-on-flow into a usable delay-robustness method.

Table 4: LIBERO per-suite results under varying delay (success rate %, 500 episodes per suite per delay). DEFLECT matches or exceeds VLASH on every (suite, delay) cell; ∆ is the 4-suiteaveraged improvement over VLASH.

Table 3: Kinetix main results, full per-delay table (success rate %; 1024 rollouts × 12 tasks per cell). ∆ is DEFLECT−VLASH. Bolded values in the success-rate columns mark the best among the five delay-aware methods (NAIVE, VLASH, RTC, BID, DEFLECT); SYNC‡ is a no-inferencedelay reference and is excluded from this rule. RTC (inpainting) and BID (closed-loop resampling) both rely on the residual chunk prefix from the previous prediction; this prefix shrinks with d (chunk length 8 minus d) and becomes too short to support either method at d ≥ 5, at which point both collapse. VLASH itself remains functional throughout the sweep but degrades; DEFLECT further improves on VLASH everywhere, with the largest margins in the extreme-delay regime.
